The Irish Industrial Development Association are to
be congratulated on securing a conviction against
Messrs. John Arnott and Co., Itd., of Belfast, for sell-
‘ing, as Irish, paper that wa.s not Trish. Arnott and
Co., of Belfast, ought to feel that they were very lightly
Sk ' dealt with_ by bemg only fined £5 for this grievous and
¥ -killing offence. On the 31st August last they

E. J. Riordan some fore1gn paper labelled
Trish poplm 77 a8 Trish-made paper. Our readers may
emember that some time back we drew attention to
lin ** note-paper made abroad.  Since

appears that Messrs. Arnott and Co.,
‘placed a notice in their window that * Trish
as paper of English manufacture gna.de for

for the green Irish market they

However, the chief point is -that bcforc
nott and Co., of Belfast, sold

The assistant told Mr. Riordan that the paper was
Irish made. Mr. Riordan appears to have expressed
doubts, and anyway he asked her to go and make sure.
She cousulted some other employee of Messrs. Arnott
and Co., and subsequently repeated her statement that
the paper was Irish made. The solicitor for Arnott’s
admitted the offence, and made excuses. Well, excuses
arc not law. The assistant and the person she con-
sulted, it appears, according to the defendants’ case,
were thomselves deceived by the description on the
packets! Well, was the buyer deceived? Where did
Arnott and Co. buy the paper the description of which,
according to their solicitor’s contention, was so decep-
tive that it deceived some of Arnott’s assistants into
thinking that it was Irish manufacture! We may dis-
miss tho excuses with the remark that if the assistant
and the other person she consulted were deceived, then
it proves all the more the offence of the responsible
person or persons at Arnott’s who purchased the paper;
for it was not put forward as an oxcuse that the buyer
of the paper thought it was Irish manufacture. M.
Hodder, R.M., very properly said that he and his col-
leagues on the bench regarded the offence -as a very
scrious one. We should think it was. But is it not
a strange thing that it is left to a voluntary association
to prosecute Daddy Swindle Fingers whilst the Depart-
ment are inactive—have no powers according to their
own showing—and spend public money chasing Daddy
Long Legs.

Trinity College has an unenviable fame in relation to
the simple art of spelling. Last week we gave three
samples of questions from Intermediate Examination
papers in Geometry set by Mr. Robert Russell, M.A.
Who is Mr. Robert Russell, M.A.? A correspondent
writes to us asking if it is possible that he is the same as
the Mr. Russell who is Secretary of Bung’s Association.
We have looked up the matter, and find that it is not
so. We find that Mr. Robert Russell, M.A., is a
Junior Fellow of the so-called University of Dublin,
more popularly known now as the College Green Spell-
ing Bee. Robert Russell, M.A., who, if we may judge
by one of his quesblons, thinks that each side of a
triangle is parallel to the other sides, was elected a
Junior Fellow in 1888, some 18 years ago, so that he
has had ample time to study the nature of a triangle,
and his blunder cannot be justly extenuated on the
ground of extreme youth.

The keeping in order and repairing of the silver waré
in the Viceregal Lodge is a fairly substantial contract,
and considering the sentiments expressed by Lord Aber-
deen with reference to Irish industry, we are surprised
to hear that that work is now done by a London firm.
Formerly, and certainly in Lord Dudley’s time, it was
done, according to our information, in Dublin. We
also hear that the Viceregal catering is done, largely if
not altogether, by a firm, or firms, in London.

It is rumoured that St. Vincent’s Hospital, one of
the principal Catholic hospitals of Dublin, is about to
import an Englishwoman to fill the position of matron.
‘What is the reason of this? Are we to take it that the
method of tmmmg in Vincent's is so defective that
when a vacancy for a matron arises the post cannot be -
filled from amongst hundreds of nurses wh ha
received thexr certificates from Vincent’s?

@ advocate, and is likewise associated with the Imposition,
thinks of this application. Mr. T. W. Russell, M.P.,
whoss notoriety was largely won on the temperance
platform, announced that he would vote for the proposed
new bungery at the International Imposition! And a
Mr. M‘Cormack, described as solicitor for the Temper-
ance Party, stated that he was instructed not to offer
any objection. Solicitor for what Temperance Party
we wonder! Is it for the organisation of which Mr.
Moriarty, the bankrupt, was recently secretary? We
trust that when this application, the hearing of which
was adjourned, comes on for hearing again that the
temperance advocates will offer it most strenuous opposi-
tion.

The Grand Lodge of the Free and Accepted Masons
of Ireland entertained those journalists who are mem-
bers of that secret and pernicious society in their hall
or secret meeting-place at Molesworth Street. There
was first a “ G.L. of instruction,” whatever that is,
after which the company of over 100 brethren—we do
not learn whether they were all journalists or not—
withdrew to the dining-hall. Sir James Creed Mere-
dith, one of the paid secretaries of the Royal Univer-
sity and Deputy Grand Master of the Freemasons in
Ireland, presided. Lord Justice Fitzgibbon, an over-
paid public official, interpreted masonry and its ritual
for the benefit of the journalists in a way which we are
told is never to be forgotten by those listening to him.
The Protestant Bishop of Ossory was there with mem-
bers of the secret craft. We are not able to say whether
or mnot Brother Goulding, “gentleman,’”’ graced the
occasion with his presence.

Our readers will remember that all the five science
and technological scholarships at the Royal College of
Science, Dublin, were won this year by pupils-of Mount-
joy School. We commented on the matter in our issue
of the 8th. We do not find that Mountjoy School did
anything wonderful at the Intermediate. Judged by
the number of exhibitions and prizes it comes only thir-
teenth on the list, and is beaten by nine “ Idolatrous
schools and colleges. ~ Perhaps the Mountjoy School
people think, and they may be wise, that valuable
science scholarships are better worth winning than In-
termediate exhibitions and prizes. We hope the Catho-

| lic colleges will make a better fight for these scholar-
ships in future.

Concerning the alleged  prestige’’ of Trinity College

a correspondent, in the course of a letter to us, writes—

“ Why, the thing is hardly known outside Ireland, and

where it is known at all it is only as, say, about a tenth

rate University, or rather University College. I have

2 lived all over Great Britain—England, Wales and Scot-
2 land—and have been brought a good deal into associa-
tion with the public generally, including University
men—graduates and undergraduates. Well, T do not
think I ever heard Trinity mentioned beyond once in
all my experience, and then it was by a Welsh doctor
who had taken his degree, or at least studied there, and
he certainly seemed to take no particular pride in his
Alma Mater. Then to see things in their due propor-
tion, take up a literary or scientific or general encyclo-
pedia, run your eye over the index, and, making every
allowance for your Ushers, Wares, Berkeleys, Swifts,
Burkes, Goldsmiths, Leckys and Stokes, etc., efc., you
will see what an insignificant figure Trinity compara-

you will find far more Papist Irish, who were never inside
the walls of a University at all, represented in such
works than you will Trinity men.””  Further on our
correspondent says:— Trinity’s prestige is mostly of
an insular kind, and is only appreciated by the poor
gentility and petty upstarts—Protestant and Catholic
—more for social than educational reasons.’’

Mr. J. M. Wall, the distinguished Irish-American
journalist from New York, was treated to a popular and
H enthusiastic reception on the occasion of his recent visit
i to Boyle. A special meeting of the Boyle Town Council
was called at which an address of welcome was read by
{ Mr. Tully. Mr. Wall, in the course of his interesting
1 reply, referred incidentally to the work of the LEADER.
{ He said :— Indeed, gentlemen, it is due to the courage
and enterprise and unflagging zeal of that paper and its
determined editor—its fighting editor, I should say,
and I am sure that expression ought appeal to my friend
Tully (laughter)—to assert that no copy of the LrapEr
has appeared since it first began that could not be
d:scribed as interesting. It has revealed in more telling
manner than any other publication I know of the tre-
mendous grip which the Cromwellians and their sym-
pathisers even yet hold in Ireland.”

Mr. Wall had something interesting to say on the sub-
ject of Irish Emigration to America, and our readers
}vtll find the following extract from his speech interest-
ing:—

Pn{blic sentiment in America at this time is opposed to emi-
gration generally. Life in America is very hard. There is no
mercy in the commercial life of America. It is a great country,
bat be sure and get the two sides of the question, my friends.
Human life is held very cheaply there. Thero is none of the
* sympathetic neighbourly help you will find in Ireland.
When you land in New York you will want to bo strong, mus-
cular, and healthy. You would want to be a man that did not
3 . get drunk—or, better still, that did not drink at all; .and you
SE2 would want to be a man that two or three hard failures would
~ not knock out. The other nationalitics are coming in swarms.
Russia is sending enormous crowds of emigrants; so is Italy.
The Jews, who are so wonderfully successful in other countrics,
~ are fa:t becoming a living power there. They aro becoming the
money powor. It used to be said the Irish, politically, owned
New York; but this would be more néarly true now if said of the
Jows. These people coming from those countries come with
considerable money and technical knowledge. The averagoe
Irishman who goes over now has not a knowledge of any parti-
cular trade. He may go into the saloon business, or get em-
ployment as a bar tender, or assist in cleaning the streots, or be
~ a deck hand on a ferry boat, or a conductor on a street car, or
2 waitcr in a hotel; but the other men who come have technical
‘knowledge and sufficient money to start business, and aro not
confined to onc particular business. That is the condition which
nfronts a man going from Iroland t) America at present. In
view, therofore, my frionds, of the better possibilities which are
 opening up in_Ireland, in ‘Godls nmamo :stay in dreland.
. The of {his seotion of Ireland can show (he ability of ur

A correspondent of our contemporary, the Manéghan

THE TEADER.

83

People, comments on  Ulster’s ”’ article on Tmperial
‘Ulster ”’ in a recent issue of the Leaper. It will be
remembered that our contributor quoted - statistics
from Thom’s Directory, setting out the valuation of
each county in rolation to its population. By the way,
the correspondent of our contemporary makes a hig
blunder in quoting from the LeADER, for beyond the
figures concerning the counties, which we assume are
quoted correctly, though we have not checked them,
the other part of the quotation did not appear in tho
LEADER. We can only surmise that an article, or letter,
on similar lines, but quite differently worded, must have
appeared in some other paper, and that the correspon-
dent of our contemporary must have inadvertently sub-
stituted one for tho other. However, the point is not
very material to our present purpose.

The correspondent of our contemporary ridicules, or
attempts to ridicule, the use which “ Ulster ”’ made of
the statistics from Thom. It may, or may not, be cor-
rect that “Ulster’” can be taken up here and there on a
point. Be that as it may, we think that the valuation
per inhabitant is a legitimate test of the wealth of the
people of the counties. Ulster boasts of its wealth—
meaning 'by that term its wealth in terms of £ s. d.—
and it is, we submit, a fair and legitimate reply to show
that counties outside those of Ulster are valued per
head of population at higher figures. If the question “is,
as between two men, which is the wealthier in the com-
mercial sense, the question must be decided in terms of
money. The fact that the man with £10,000 was an
idler, and inherited his money from his papa, or from
his maiden aunt, and that the man with £500 came by
that sum as a result of an industrious life, is quite irre-
levant. The big Meath grazier who may be good for
nothing but himself, and little for that either, and
who may live in singlo blessedness, and employ only a
few gatcmen and a dog, is wealthier in terms of money
than the industrious tillage farmer who rears a family
and employs labourers, and takes many times more per
acre out of the land than the grazier does. As Ulster
boasts of its wealth, it is a fair answer to show thab
as per valuation statistics the people of its counties are
poorer per head than people in other counties. - TIf
wealth in terms of £ s. d. be the boast, then let such
wealth be the test. And judged by that test Ulster, on
being weighed, is found wanting.

The correspondent of our contemporary,-the onaghan
People, says:—* It must be obvious to the most pre-
judiced that the average amount of land per individual
is a moro reliable test of prosperity than the average
valuation, otherwise all tho talk about the emigration
evil is the veriest cant.”’ We would remark that “ pros-
perity ”’ is a somewhat different word from wealt] &
when “ wealth >’ is used as something expressed in terms
of money. We need scarcely say that we have no
brief for the big bloated mass of Anglicisation and com.--
parative uselessness that digests its food and drink
inside the clothes of what we conceive to be the typical
Meath grazier; but a thief who has robbed a thousand
pounds and holds it, is wealthier in terms of £s. d.
than the honest-man who has only :£100. We wou}‘d
like to see every acre of ground in Meath maintain its
man, but when Ulster boasts of the money of its people,
particularly of its Unionists, even the Meath people
are quite entitled to say that they, as judged by valua-
tion statistics, are the richest community in Ireland in
terms of money. The dotards of Trinity College, who
fatten on the endowments of the institution, are far
and away more wealthy than the men who have made
the various Irish Training Colleges, and they remain
wealthior in the money sense in spite of the fact that
they are blots on the country, whilst those who worked
fho Irish Training'Colleges are ornaments to it.

Father Matt Ryan, P.P., of Kmockavilla, Co. Tip-
perary, advertises in our current issue for an Irish-
speaking teacher. We draw editorial attention to the
fact, as within the past four and a half years Father
Matt has made six appointments to the position of
principal or assistant teacher in the schools. The appoint-
ment he is now about to make will be the seventh of
a native Irish speaker. Certainly he is doing one man’s
part in the cause of the Irish language. On a recent
Sunday Dr. Douglas Hyde showed his appreciation of
the work done by Father Matt Ryan by attending and
speaking at his Feis at Donaskeigh, an outlying district
of his parish. The boys of Knockavilla performed
Father Dinneen’s play, Tobap Opasordeacca > in admir-
able fashion, and Miss Sighle Hallisey sang, and Miss
Cait, O’Donoghue recited. —Indeed, so pleased was
Father Matt Ryan with the performance of these two
ladies that he specially engaged them to sing and recite
at every Feis that he will have during the next three
years.  That sort of practical applause may well be
appreciated by Miss Hallisey and Miss O’Donoghue.

Sir,—Sinco my last lelter to you, I have been in oloso correspon-
denco with an old schoolmato of mine on the subject of omigravien
and kindred matters. My friend resides in tho County Wisklow.

GIRLS' TRAINING COLLEGES.

Daar SiR,—I am glad you have called attention to a matter of
no inconsiderablo injustioe on the part of the heads of some of our
training colleges—that, namely, of showing, favouritism in the
selection for training of King’s scholars.

The showing favouritism, in the ovent of a publio examination,
is a thing which no one can justify. Thero is a young girl from
the country, for instance, preparing, for oight months of the year,
for tho ontrance examinations to Carysfort Park Training College.
Sho passed creditably and is awaiting anxiously to be called for
training when, lo and behold, she sees another girl, an acquaint-
ance of hers, who has attained fewer marks, called, while she is
rejectod : with the result, that she has to prepare anew for next
year’s examinations, and compete with newcomers on a now set of
subjects. And why all this? Because, forsooth, tho second girl
was attending one of tho Sister’s schools, or the school of the
members of another institute to whom the authorities of the
oollege wish to pay a compliment.

Nor can the authorities at the Education Office interfere. They
aro as helpless in the righting of the injustice done as they are
alleged to be in the case of funds for educational purposes sup-
posed to be under the control of the Treasury officials.

Nor is the Blackrock institution the only offender in this matter.
There is another training college, further north, the heads of
which were guilty of a flagrant pieco of favouritiem, to my own
kunowledge, not many years ago. The case was this:—A couple
of teachers of most excellent character teaching in a school with
which I had some connection, were denied admittance for train-
ing, when, I was aware, that monitors of the fifth year, from the
same lown, were received, though they had received fewer marks,
and though, according to the intention of the Education Board,
teachors holding a position are to be given preference over
monitors, even though they make a less brilliant examination.

HERE is a bold, bad article in this month’s
T Independent Review on ‘‘ Michael Davitt’s Un-
Snished Campaign.”” The ‘ campaign’’ is—or was—
against the clergy and the clericals, and had for its aim
the establishment of a perfectly new Democracy (with a
very big “D’) in Ireland. The writer—need we say

- it is Mr. F. Sheehy-Skeffington?—apparently thinks
the mantle of Michael has descended upon him, and he
writes accordingly. He sees clericalism in everything.
Here are a few of his phrases: ‘ priestly dictation B>
¢ dictatorial clericalism ”’; clerical power’’; the
episcopal whip ’’; ‘“the episcopal lash’’; clerical

* hands’’; ““ the arrogant spirit of clericalism *; ““ sub-

servience to a priestly hint’’; ‘ priestly prurience’’;
our clerical rulers’’; ¢ clerical terrorists’’; ‘‘the
thumb of the Bishops.” Tremendous fellows, these
priests! The little garland of phrases culled from the
articlo of Mr. S.-S. will sufficiently show how far that
gentleman is fitted for taking a philosophically dispas-
sionate view of our priests.

But what is the article all about? It is not easy to
say. It seems an attempt to foreshadow the future of
Irish politics, culminating at some indefinite period
with the triumph of the new Democracy. Much of the
article is a survey of the contents of what we are told
was Mr. Davitt’s last review article, and the prophecies
contained therein. In this article it was assumed that
Home Rule being obtained, a Conservative and Clerical
Ministry headed by Mr. John Redmond takes office;
in it are supposed to be included Mr. William O’Brien,
Sir Horace Plunkett—and Mr. Waldron! Well, the
whole new Democracy is to unite in putting this
Ministry out of power, said Democracy consisting of a
combination of the Independent Orangemen, the
National Democrats, and the Labour Members, led by
Mr. Dillon, Mr. Davitt, Mr. Lindsay Crawford, and
Mr. Walker, of Belfast. To this combination Mr.
S.-S. gives the auspicious title of the Democratic
‘“Bloc,”” thereby adopting the special title of the
French Radical Secularists. Mr. S.-S. calls this the
most interesting part of Mr. Davitt’s last article; the
overthrow of the Redmond-Conservative Ministry is
supposed to take place at the first elections under the
new system—the first presumably after a dissolution.
Who the National Democrats precisely are, we are not
told ; evidently they are to be anti-clerical. Mr. John
Redmond can hardly be one of them, as he is described
as the ‘‘most obedient servitor’ of the priests.
Apparently, too, anyone who wants to keep step with
the new Democracy will want to be very democratic in-
deed. Here is Mr. Skeffington complaining that the

MISS EKENNEDY'S EDITION OF TENNYSON’S
“MORTE D’ARTHUR.”

HAT there is a Catholic point of view in treating
of literature appears unknown to most of us.

Even Walter Pater, most charming of critics, thought

that St. Bernard typified the attitude of the Church

towards letters, when he described him riding on his
mule by the Lake of Geneva with his head bent down,
his cowl drawn over it, and his meditations on the
wickedness of the world, whilst the glory of the ever-
lasting hills and the sheen of the shining valleys and the
melodious music of the birds were all unheeded. In
coarser style, a lesser sage, Richard Le Gallieni, sums
up the Church not as the nursling mother of the arts,
but as “ ordinary humanity—in a surplice.” - One of
the greabest of the Victorian poets crystallises his view
of the Catholic ideal as a saint who could “stink and
starve.”” Nothing could be more alien from the real atti-
tude of Catholicity. A recent writer remarks that the
emotion of Shelley is as refined as any in the realm of
song; yet he was not a Christian. Now both Keats and

. Shelley are really exponents of the Catholic Philosophy
of Art. They were Greeks at heart—all truth is
beauty, and all beauty is truth,’” is alike their creed, and
the key-note of the attitude of the Church Philosophy
towards Art. The Idealism of Plato, modified by Axis-
totle ‘and sanctified by Aquinas, taught that beauty was
the effort of the created thing to manifest its meta-
=~ physical reality—to show forth the ideal in the maker’s
mind ‘according to which he fashioned it and, which
it falls short of only because of subluminary limitations.

Though the LEADER is mostly concerned to help the
Irish-Ireland movement as it exhibits itself in Gaelic
literature, it must not overlook the Irish intellect ex-
. pressing itself in the, alas! more familiar language of
the stranger. The peculiar genius of the Irish, at least
of the Irish of the Pale, is seen at its best when it seeks
to sympathetically expound the great leaders of Eng-
lish literature and to interpret them in a spirit at once
Irish and Catholic.

After all, the Gaelic movement, grand as it is, is
limited to Ireland. Those who treat of letters in a
Catholic spirit have a wider audience, and Miss Mary
Olivia Kennedy, who has lately published an edition of
Tennyson’s “ Morte D’Arthur,”’ appeals to an audience
extending from the rising to the setting sun.

With the Intermediate a new soul came into Erin,
but those of us who remember the editions of the classic
authors, put into the hands of the students in its early
days, will recollect what a surfeit of Sourfaces we had
in the editors.” One’s early imagination was shown a
great author distorted through the medium of an in-
telligence agnostic, offensive, or at least highly Protes-
tant.” Now we begin to realise our strength, and even the
canvent schools can show editors whose texts deserve the
description of Addison—meruwm sal—a delicious little
thing.  Their beauties are enhanced ; their philosophy
is corrected in the Catholic sense; their meaning is elu-
cidated with sympathetic insight.

The ““Death of Arthur’’ is one of Tennyson’s most
delightful fragments, . but is is simply unintelligible
unless lucidly edited. The legend, woven from the old
tale of Malory, -showing the . Knights of the Round
Table, models of: chivalry all, and their heroic king,

who receives- his treacherous death-wound and meets -

his fate, is a characteristically Celtic romance which is

. absorbed into English literature with as much ease as
if it were a new country absorbed into the Empire.
There is a picturesques grip in Miss Kennedy’s account

. of the smug British spirit in which Tennyson’s Idyll
was first received. :
“The early Victorians were not prepared for a repeti-
tion of the innocent outspokenness of-the early romances,
or of that feudal morality which runs through Malory’s
work, and is said to have even appeared exaggerated in
the last half of the fifteenth century. The Cymric Ate,
the cumulative effects of the working out of the curse
which the by-no-means-blameless Arthur of the

~ romance innocently incwrred, and sank under at the
battle of Camlan, would have proved quite unsuitable to

THE LEADER.

. window-sills, stools are brought out;

89

the delicate pen of the poet or to the newly-sensitive
ears of his age. Consequently the ‘daemonic’ spirit
which in broad tale of the old chroniclers finds its ter-
rible, but reasonable, culmination in the slaying of
Arthur by Mordred, is lost, and a very ordinary attempt
at regicide is given in its stead.’’

Not necessarily is the lady trained in the convent
2 namby-pamby miss, and Miss Kennedy treats the
characterisation of Arthur with a vigour and a vim which
is more than masculine. Of the king she says:— Not-
withstanding his occasional exhibition of despotic anger,
the Arthur of Malory is a far more interesting figure
than the self-restrained, emasculated, depressed being
who lurks beh'nd the knightly figures in the Idylls, and
who only makes a manly appearance on a few occasions
to be found in ‘The Coming of Arthur,” ‘Balin and
Balin,” and ‘The Passing of Arthur.” Tennyson’s
‘ Blameless King’ is perhaps seen at his worst in the
scene with Genevieve, where he reproaches her in a tone
of self-conscious rectitude which even a ‘ stainless gentle-
man’ of the nineteenth century might consider a trifle
overdone. Furnivall, in his preface to ‘The Saint
Graal,” says, if Arthur did this ‘he was the Pecksniff
of the age.” In general outline, then, the Arthur of
the Idylls is not the dramatic figure of Malory’s
Morte.””

There is scarcely a line in this Idyll of Tennyson’s
which does not call forth the like vivid and picturesque
comment from Miss Kennedy’s pen. The work is pre-
scribed for various examinations, and contains not only
a sketch of Tennyson’s life, but a succinct digest of his
merits as a poet and a luminous appreciation of his place
in English letters. Published at the price of a few pence
it can easily challenge comparison with any of the
classical editions issuing from the Oxford University
Press. IDOLATOR.

REFLECTIONS IN SUBURBIA.

EADERS of Gorky’s book, “Creatures that
Once Were Men,” will remember with what a
heart the unfortunate ‘“ creatures ’—the very dregs of
humanity—used to set to philosophise on every ques-’
tion that turned into their path. Leisure and irrespon-
sibility were theirs: and given these, discussions may
well be endless, though at first glance one might think
that the very poor would have something else to trouble
them. But so is life. There’s not a lane or alley but
has its school of politics; and the most vivid argu-
ments take place among scaffold-poles during dinner
hour. In these places conversationalists are at pre-
mium ; keen intellects are valued; and the mason never
works the witty labourer as he works the clod. So in
all spheres of life talent finds rewards.

How different it is in Suburbia! The “cold chain
of silence ’’ must hang on this land of villa residences.
Behold them!—not one without its plot of green and
handful of flowers inviting the family out of doors, one
would think, on summer evenings. ~But no, as silent
in the dusk as in mid-day when the master was at
work, the missis shopping, and the juniors at school.
Wherefore the silence? Leisure is there, but irrespon-
sibility 7  There’s the lack! Do you not know that
the people next door are spectres?

Imagine a rout of lane-dwellers suddenly transformed
into suburbians. They gather to door-steps and
7 ) > the Murphys
fraternise with the Gilligans; what talk ascends to the
moon in the warm night? music has its way now and
then, and rough chorusing; and all surely no offence
to God or man.

Now when Suburbia goes to the sea-side it is mot
altogether different from that. And consequently ceas-
ing to dwell in boredom, it becomes a refreshing sight
to men’s eyes, with its sun-burnt brows and unsophis-
ticated blouses. Bub for all that it is not altogether
beautiful, for with these violent efforts towards relaxa-
tion it tends to the other extreme, and goes near the
verge of bounce. Practice is needful before taking a fling,
even an annual fling, at the sea-side. On the fine evenings

of egxly summer we think the suburbians might begin by
‘coming out into the air, by having their talks, by read-
ing their books. in becoming seaside postures, but, of
_ cowrse, song and music must remain taboo!

Not alone for the sake of future figures to be cut
ab the sea-side, but for all concerned we think the in-
habitants of Suburbia ought listen to this advice. It may
help ‘the neighbourhood to regain some of the cheerful-
ness it lost when “ To Let for Building *’ first appeared
on a notice board stuck in a hedge. Such a road we
knew. There were odd cottages on it, some white-
washed, others washed with buff, trees in twos

_and threes, gateways broken and mended, bits of
hedge alternating with bits of wall, and cart-tracks cut-
ting across the pathways—indeed, a very ordinary road.
But the sun occasionally, and the moon, whenever the
moon was there, used play tricks with that roadway,
used mix up broken and mended, bush and tree, white
and buff in such a way that anyone with eyes for such
things stopped again and again just for a ha’p’orth of
look about. I wonder could a dozen suns and as many
moons prevent a road. of cemented villa residences from
being the rigid thing it is. But this is not what we
were thinking of when we set out.

Does this silence, this rigidity, this cast-iron correct-*

ness get inside the houses? ~We are afraid of it.
Timidity is the cause of it all; and this cannot be
shaken off even when out of view of the neighbours.
Is it true that the inhabitants of Suburbia say “ Thank
God *’ with the usual sigh of relief every time they get
inside their hall-doors out of sight? One can imagine
it. Anyway it is certain that the spirit of adventure
dwells not in the realms of bow-windows. Now the
lower classes are not so. They have their packs of
beagles, and, following the dogs’ most tunable cry, they
rush headlong into bogs, plunge through thickets,
break into demesne-lands, dash through streams, and in
a word get their blood to the boiling point. And they
have many other such. sports. The upper classes, too,
have ways of their own by means of which they run
danger of dippings in streams on cold days, sometimes
even gefting to that state of hot-bloodedness which
I makes nothing of a broken collar-bone. Poor Suburbia!
How far off it is from. all that! It is really further
than it needs be. We can understand it if it lifts its
eyebrows at the sight of a broken back, but why must
Suburbia forswear the fields altogether? No one ever
met a suburbian in the fields. He keeps to the path-
ways that the Co. Councils keep nice for him.  Yet
the fields are the places for stars, and streams, and
birds, and flowers and dews—much more than can he
pub into words—‘‘elves and the harmless glamour of the
i field.”” Did. the ancestor of the suburbian never dwell
I in tents? Thinking of these things, the pathway youth
§ of Suburbia cuts but a poor figure in our mind. Yea,
‘Gorky’s phrase is a great one—* Creatures that Once
Were Men.”’ 5

It is all significant. A group of Gaelic Leaguers
lately stood in a district of Suburbia. As usual with
such men their talk was of the years that are to be. The
very humblest Gaelic Leaguer knows what ideals are;
he knows how. they make. sad rather than happy, how
strangely the mind of man is whipped rather than
.caressed by them—yet he, the very humblest, would nof
put them by. Such the mind of our group. One of
them stopped and stood upright.  “That's all very
'sickening,”” he said, and took in terrace after terrace
with a sweep of his arm. And it was sickening. Nob
an Irishman, as far as. we knew, in any house of all the

land, and which has no doubt caused the Irish athletes
to take action, should be the medium of a further effort
to bring reason to bear on the governing body of the
G.A.A., and indirectly on the other Irish Athletic Asso-
ciations.

At present athletes who compete at meetings under
G.A.A. laws are precluded from competing at
meetings under the laws of either the Irish Cyclists’
Association or the Irish Amateur Athletic Association
and vice versa, and as the English and Scotch Associa-
tions recognise the suspensions of the I.C.A. and

barred: from competing in the English and' ‘Scotch
championships. Any reasonable Irishman: will at once
conclude: that such a state of affairs should not be
ailowed to: continue,. because it narrows the sphere of
athletic progress, and prevents many good Irishmen
from exercising: their rights.

I have no hesitation in saying that the members. of
the governing body of the G.A.A., who are responsible
for the rule precluding soldiers and policemen from
competing at athletic meetings or playing games under
G.A.A. laws, have done a grave injury to their
country.  Ivishmen: do not join. either the army or
police' force from choice, but from necessity; and,
although. both. systems of service tend to quench any
sparks. of Irish. nationality: in the breasts of those who
join them, it would be a most unwise proceeding for
any body calling itself Irish to.adopt means to actually
prevent any chance of fanning into flame the dormant
or dying' embers of Irish nationdlity which. remain in
the heart of the soldier or policeman. In fact T
would go.so far as. to say that there are many as good
Irishmen in the army and police as are to be found in
the G.A.A. Such a policy drives. many a good athlete
and Irishman over to the enemy. If a young Irishman
cannot obtain. work in. Ireland he must either emigrate,
join the police or army, or go to the workhouse. The
first requires money, which he has not got,. the second
requires time and even.influence, and he must get food:;
the. workhouse he: generally abhors, and it. takes. money.
from. the ratepayers, and there remains the army! Is
that his fault, or is it his misfortune, and is: he a. worse
Irishman on: that account ? :

It is a well-known fact that the Gaelic League is
open. to: all classes. of Irvishmen. In fact, it is. open to
all people; who wish. to. learn the.language of Ireland,
and: who wish. to study its history and promote its
material and moral: welfare. Why: should. the. G.A.A.
adopt, a different atiitude to the Gaelic League 2

Nearly every. member of the Gaelic. League who is
phygically fit: is a member of the G.A.A., but E. ventur:
tor say that, five-sixths of the. members of the G
are not members of the Gaelic League.. If the gowe;
ing body of the G:A.A. would induce the: clubs: unde:
its control to form Gaelic League classes and supp

home industries instead of passing rules to exclude

There is another aspect of the case. Every Irishman

A CONSTELLATION OF IRISH POETS.

Filrve na Mage, the Maigue Poets, edited by
Father Dinneen. Gill and Son, Dublin.

HERE can be little doubt that Father Dinneen’s
edition of the Maigue Poets is, in many respects,
the most interesting, and in every respect the most use-
ful, volume of Irish poetry that has ever appeared in
print. In quality the poetry in the volume does not
rank as high as that of O’Rahilly, which became so
popular immediately on its appearance. The poems of
the Maigue Poets are not as rich or as splendid as those
of Eoghan Ruadh O’Sullivan. But they have a certain
adyantage over the productions of the poets just men-
tioned, they are largely concerned with topics of every-
day life. They are short and pointed and unconven-
tional. They arouse interest by their local and per-
sonal allusions. They present to us, as in a mirror,
the ordinary life of certain classes of the people during
the middle years of the eighteenth century.

In his preface to O’Rahilly’s poems, Father Dinneen
predicted for the book a popularity which was abund-
antly verified. I think it safe to predict for the collec-
tion of the works of the Maigue Poets now under notice
a still wider popularity and a greater hold on the
general reader. — The editor in the present instance,
schooled, no doubt, by experience, has gone a long way
to meet the difficulties of student readers on whom the
sale of a book of this kind so largely depends. There
are many students of Irish throughout the country in
private classes, in Gaelic Loague classes, and engaged
in private study who have not the benefit of competent
teachers, and who make heroic efforts to struggle
through Irish poetry. Their success is not in propor-
tion- to their zeal. The reason is that a good deal of
the poetry within their reach is too difficult, and they
have no very satisfactory means of solving the difficulties
they meet. Nay, there are many persons appointed to
teach Irish poetry who feel ashamed to face their
class, unable as they are to answer the questions which
the most: commonplace members of their classes may

_ hurl at them at any moment. Father Dinneen has in
this edition wisely come to the rescue of these students
and teachers. In an admirable series of notes, which
are largely pithy translations of difficult passages, he
has succeeded inenabling the ordinary student not only

' to read intelligently, but to appreciate the beauties of

these exquisite poems. 3

i

SEPTEMBER 29, 1906.

The book is admirably arranged and finely produced.
The introduction, which is mostly in English, gives so
vivid a picture of eighteenth century life in County
Limerick and the adjoining district of County Cork
that on reading it we imagine ourselves seated in
O’Tuomy’s parlour, with a crowd (_)f wits and poets,
while songs, stories, extempore lyric effusions, biting
satirical repartees, beguile the winter’s night, or we are
transported in spirit to the tent in the fair field while
tho flood approaches with menacing aspect, and the
poet spadesman beguiles his labour with a caustic' re-
mark destined soon to be fruitful of results. We imagine
we see the Mangaire Sugach, the droll, the witty, the
melodious Mangaire ever grumbling at his fate, ever
plotting some new satirical onslaught on O’Tuomy, ever
gay and jovial in the company of the bards. We pic-
ture to ourselves the Lios at Bruree where the Sessions
of the Bardic Court used to be held.  The reverent
attentive audience that listened bare-headed as the greal
Sean Clarech thundered forth his fierce satires against
bigots and tyrants, and that punctuated his stanzas
with rounds of applause. We listen with the crowd
breathlessly as O’Tuomy’s sweet voice chants one
of his great and faultless lyrics.  The melody comes
floating on the breeze as the refrain of some fairy
song.  Our souls glow with the rapture begotten of
such a scene, and we are disposed to reflect when our
reverie is ended, what a deep irreparable loss our
country has suffered in the decay of the Irish language
and the neglect which became the lot of our glorious
literature.

The introduction, it is safe to assert, will be as
popular as the works of the poets with which it deals,
and deserves to be studied by the historian and anti-
quarian as affording a unique picture of Irish life at a
period when the Penal Laws were doing their utmost
to banish learning and gaiety from our shores, and to
unman and degrade the Irish race. Here we have an
irrefragable proof of the buoyancy of the Irish spirit, of
the dogged tenacity of our race in the face of black
discouragement and overwhelming odds. This introduc-
tion is, for its scope, an important historical chapter in
the history of Ireland in the eighteenth century.

Coming to the poems themselves, which extend over
144 pages of the volume, we find that they are mainly
those of John O’Tuomy, of Croom, and of Andrew
M‘Grath, the life-long friend of O’Tuomy. The works
of the other Maigue Poets are introduced only in so far
as they have replied to either O’Tuomy or M‘Grath, or
vice versa. Thus Father Nicholas O’Donnell, the Guar-
dian of the Franciscan Monastery of Adare, sets the
entire Maigue poetic concert singing by leading off with
a humorous lament for his horse, which was supposed
to be done to death by being ““overlooked ”” by a red-
haired woman, or “stolen ’’ by the fairies that ‘dwelt
in the recesses of some of Limerick’s green and gently-
s'oping hills. Another topic that provoked many prac-
tical epistles was the “conversion’ into a Protestant
Minister of a well-known Dominican Friar named Hed-
derman. But the most exciting poetic contests are
those which are in the nature of duels in verse between
O'Tuomy and M‘Grath. We rise from reading  these
“duels”’ doubtful as to which the palm belongs, but
convinced that the pair of combatants could mot easily
be rivalled in any land. We are inclined to think that
O’Tuomy wrote much more than has come down to us.
He lived a long life. He began to write poetry early;
he seems to have continued to.write poetry to the
last years of his life. It is hardly possible that all he
ever composed has been preserved. The elegies that
commemorate his death are couched in an unwonted
strain, and seem to point to a rich harvest of poetry.
Wha.t, remains to us of that harvest is certainly a pre-
cious relic, and stamps the author not only as a man
of lyrical and satirical genius, but also as a strong
character and an upright Catholic Irishman. 7

The Mangaire Sugach, as M‘Grath was usually called,
though of a less stern mould, was withal bold in his
denunciation of the foreign tyrants, and did his part to
keep alive the true Irish and Cabholic spirit in the
breast of his countrymen. Around this pair of disti

guished poets there olustered others of less note, men of
